content.body:
Electric current I: rate of flow of charge
I = Q/t  (A = C/s)
Q = charge (coulombs, C), t = time (s)

Voltage (potential difference) V:
Work done per unit charge  V = W/Q
Unit: volt (V)

Resistance R:
Ohm's Law: V = IR
Unit: ohm (Ω)

Power:
P = IV = I²R = V²/R
Unit: watt (W)

content.title: Kirchhoff's Laws
content.body:
KCL (current law): sum of currents into junction = sum leaving
I₁ = I₂ + I₃

KVL (voltage law): sum of voltages around any closed loop = 0
In series: V_total = V₁ + V₂ + V₃
In parallel: V_total = V₁ = V₂ = V₃ (same voltage)

content.title: Series and Parallel
content.body:
Series: R_total = R₁+R₂+R₃ (same current)
Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁+1/R₂+1/R₃ (same voltage)

Two 6Ω in series: R=12Ω
Two 6Ω in parallel: 1/R=1/6+1/6=1/3, R=3Ω

questions.question: Electric current measures
questions.explanation: Current I = Q/t: amount of charge passing a point per second. Unit: ampere (A).

questions.question: Charge flowing when 3A flows for 20s
Ohm's Law states
questions.explanation: V = IR. Ohm's Law: voltage = current × resistance.

questions.question: Resistance of component with V=12V, I=0.5A
Power dissipated by 10Ω resistor with 2A
Three 3Ω resistors in series: total R
questions.explanation: Series: R = 3+3+3 = 9Ω

questions.question: Two 12Ω resistors in parallel: total R
In a parallel circuit, voltage across each branch is
questions.explanation: Parallel circuit: all branches share the same terminal voltage.

questions.question: Kirchhoff's current law states
options: sum of currents entering junction = sum leaving
questions.answer: sum of currents entering junction = sum leaving
questions.explanation: KCL: charge conservation — all current entering a node must leave it.

questions.question: Energy used by 60W bulb in 5 hours
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Mains electricity in Uganda is 240V AC, 50Hz. Safe use requires understanding of wiring, fuses, circuit breakers, and earthing.

UK/Uganda mains:
Voltage: 230-240V RMS
Frequency: 50Hz
Live wire: brown (carries high voltage)
Neutral wire: blue (return path, ~0V)
Earth wire: green/yellow (safety)

Fuse: thin wire that melts if current too high
Choose fuse slightly above normal operating current
3A: small electronics; 5A: medium; 13A: high-power appliances

content.title: Safety Features
content.body:
Fuse: breaks circuit if current exceeds rating → prevents fire
Circuit breaker (MCB): like fuse but reusable, trips instantly
RCCD (residual current device): detects imbalance between live/neutral → trips if person touches live wire
Earthing: connects metal parts to earth → fault current flows safely to earth → fuse blows

content.title: Electrical Energy and Units
content.body:
Energy = Power × time
Electricity bill unit: kilowatt-hour (kWh)
1 kWh = 1000W × 3600s = 3,600,000 J = 3.6 MJ

Cost = power (kW) × time (h) × price per kWh

A 2kW heater running 3h: 6 kWh
At 500 UGX/kWh: 3000 UGX

questions.question: In Uganda/UK mains, the live wire is
questions.explanation: Live wire (high voltage, dangerous): brown. Neutral (return): blue. Earth (safety): green/yellow.

questions.question: A fuse protects a circuit by
options: melting to break circuit if current is too high
questions.answer: melting to break circuit if current is too high
questions.explanation: Fuse wire has low melting point. Excess current → wire heats → melts → circuit breaks → prevents overheating/fire.

questions.question: Which fuse is best for a 750W hair dryer on 250V?
questions.explanation: I = P/V = 750/250 = 3A. Choose fuse slightly above: 3A (or 5A if no 3A available).

questions.question: The earth wire's purpose is
options: safely conduct fault current to earth → blow fuse
questions.answer: safely conduct fault current to earth → blow fuse
questions.explanation: If live wire touches metal casing, earth wire provides low-resistance path → large current → fuse blows immediately.

questions.question: 1 kWh equals
An RCCB/RCD protects against
options: electric shock — detects imbalance between live/neutral currents
questions.answer: electric shock — detects imbalance between live/neutral currents
questions.explanation: RCD detects tiny current difference between live and neutral (indicating leakage through person) and trips in milliseconds.

questions.question: Cost of running 100W bulb for 10 hours at 500 UGX/kWh
questions.explanation: Energy = 0.1kW × 10h = 1kWh. Cost = 1 × 500 = 500 UGX

questions.question: Double insulation means
options: the appliance has no earth wire because the plastic casing cannot conduct electricity
questions.answer: the appliance has no earth wire because the plastic casing cannot conduct electricity
questions.explanation: Doubly insulated appliances have no metal casing to earth — plastic insulation provides complete protection.

questions.question: Mains frequency in Uganda is
questions.explanation: Uganda (and most of Africa, UK, Europe) uses 50Hz AC mains. USA uses 60Hz.

questions.question: A circuit breaker (MCB) differs from a fuse because
options: it can be reset after tripping (reusable) whereas fuse must be replaced
questions.answer: it can be reset after tripping (reusable) whereas fuse must be replaced
questions.explanation: MCBs trip electromagnetically or thermally and are reset by flicking a switch. Fuses must be replaced after blowing.

content.body:
Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R
Units: Watts (W)

Electrical Energy:
E = Pt = IVt
Units: Joules (J)

Commercial unit: kilowatt-hour (kWh)
1 kWh = 3,600,000 J

content.title: Power Calculation
content.body:
A 240V heater draws 5A current.
P = IV = 240 × 5 = 1200W = 1.2kW
Energy in 2 hours: E = Pt = 1200 × 7200 = 8,640,000 J = 2.4 kWh
Electrical appliances are rated by power (watts). A 60W bulb uses 60J every second. Cost of electricity is based on energy used in kWh.

questions.question: Electrical power P =
questions.explanation: P = IV = current × voltage

questions.question: A 240V device draws 2A. Power =
Power formula using resistance only: P =
questions.explanation: P = I²R (derived from P=IV and V=IR)

questions.question: 1 kWh equals
A 2kW heater runs for 3 hours. Energy used =
questions.explanation: E = Pt = 2 × 3 = 6 kWh

questions.question: Which uses most power
questions.explanation: Higher wattage = more power = faster energy use.

questions.question: Fuse rating should be
questions.explanation: Fuse protects circuit - should blow if current exceeds normal operating current.

questions.question: A 100W bulb on 240V. Current =
Cost of running 3kW heater for 4 hours at 500 UGX/kWh
questions.explanation: Energy = 3×4 = 12kWh. Cost = 12 × 500 = 6000 UGX

questions.question: Short circuit is dangerous because
questions.explanation: Short circuit bypasses resistance, R→0, so I=V/R becomes extremely large, causing overheating.

content.body: Electrical safety involves understanding earthing, fuses, circuit breakers, and proper wiring. A three-pin plug has Live (brown), Neutral (blue), and Earth (green/yellow) wires.

Fuse: thin wire that melts when current too high
Circuit breaker: switch that trips automatically
Earthing: connects metal case to ground (0V)
RCD: detects current imbalance, trips quickly

content.title: Why Earth Wire Matters
content.body:
If live wire touches metal kettle case and no earth:
- Case becomes live (240V)
- Person touching case gets shock
With earth wire: fault current flows to earth, fuse blows, protecting person

questions.question: In 3-pin plug, live wire colour is
questions.explanation: In modern wiring: Live=Brown, Neutral=Blue, Earth=Green/Yellow.

questions.question: Earth wire function is to
options: Provide path for fault current to ground
questions.answer: Provide path for fault current to ground
questions.explanation: Earth wire safely conducts fault current away if live wire touches metal casing.

questions.question: Fuse is connected in
questions.explanation: Fuse must be in live wire to isolate device from dangerous voltage.

questions.question: Circuit breaker advantage over fuse
questions.explanation: Circuit breakers can be reset; fuses must be replaced after blowing.

questions.question: Double insulation means device has
options: No metal parts exposed + two layers of insulation
questions.answer: No metal parts exposed + two layers of insulation
questions.explanation: Doubly insulated devices don't need earth wire - two insulating barriers prevent shock.

questions.question: RCD (Residual Current Device) works by
options: Comparing current in live and neutral wires
questions.answer: Comparing current in live and neutral wires
questions.explanation: RCD detects difference between live and neutral currents (sign of fault) and trips instantly.

questions.question: Neutral wire is at approximately
questions.explanation: Neutral wire is maintained near earth potential (0V).

questions.question: Overloading a socket is dangerous because
options: Total current may exceed safe rating, causing fire
questions.answer: Total current may exceed safe rating, causing fire
questions.explanation: Multiple high-power devices share same wiring - total current may exceed fuse/cable rating.

questions.question: Correct fuse for device using 3A normally
questions.explanation: Choose fuse slightly above normal current. 5A fuse protects a 3A device while allowing normal operation.

questions.question: Electric shock occurs when
options: Current passes through body to earth
questions.answer: Current passes through body to earth
questions.explanation: Shock requires current path through body. Touching live wire while earthed creates this path.

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Current Electricity. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Current Electricity concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Current Electricity builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Current Electricity. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Current Electricity. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Current Electricity include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Current Electricity. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
